title: NSW looks at new levy for disasters
full_text:
Insurers have backed a NSW Treasury plan that would save households in flood-prone regions $565 a year in premiums by increasing charges on the top 5 per cent of land by value, including residential land worth more than $1.95 million.
The state parliament is considering five options to change the emergency services levy (ESL) proposed by the NSW Treasury to shift from a surcharge on insurance, which adds to underinsurance, to a general land tax.
NSW is the last mainland state relying on an insurance-based levy, which adds up to 18 per cent on top of household insurance premiums and up to 34 per cent for businesses.
In an April discussion paper, NSW Treasury proposed five options to replace it: two based on fixed charges with discounts for the regions or surcharges for certain property types; and three based on fixed costs with tiers by land value. All models propose that the majority of households will be better off, with an estimated $65 per year saving on average per household.
The Insurance Council of Australia backed a six-tier model, in which households in the cheapest quarter by land value pay $141 a year, and those in the top 5 per cent pay $623.
Levies for the ICA-backed option range from $175 to $17,972 for commercial properties; $275 to $50,857 for industrial properties; and $375 to $3280 for farms.
According to ICA-commissioned analysis by Lateral Economics, households in disaster-prone regional areas would save about $565 a year.
Small and medium businesses would see the largest savings, for example from $105 a year for a small bakery; to $8850 for a restaurant; and more than $21,000 for a small livestock farm.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Karp
heading: General Insurance
